title: Pogostemon quadrifolius
uses: Pogostemon quadrifolius is used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including treating fever, rheumatism, and other inflammatory conditions. The plant is valued for its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to soothe and calm irritated tissues. In some cultures, it is used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and indigestion. The plant's extracts have also been studied for their potential antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. Additionally, Pogostemon quadrifolius is used in some herbal remedies to support the immune system and promote overall well-being.
taxonomy: Pogostemon quadrifolius is classified in the family Lamiaceae, commonly known as the mint family. This classification places Pogostemon quadrifolius within the order Lamiales. The family Lamiaceae is a large and diverse group of plants that also includes basil, mint, and rosemary. Pogostemon quadrifolius is further classified as a genus within the family, alongside other species that share similar characteristics. The exact classification of Pogostemon quadrifolius may vary depending on the source, but it is generally accepted as a member of the Lamiaceae family.

constituents: Pogostemon quadrifolius, also known as the Javan patchouli, has been found to contain various active constituents. These constituents include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpenoids, which contribute to the plant's medicinal and aromatic properties. The flavonoids present in Pogostemon quadrifolius are known for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. Additionally, the plant's essential oil contains a unique combination of patchoulol and pogostone, which are responsible for its distinct aroma and therapeutic benefits.

intro: Pogostemon quadrifolius, also known as Patchouli, is a perennial herb belonging to the family Lamiaceae, native to Southeast Asia. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties, providing relief from skin irritations, wounds, and infections, as well as exhibiting potential benefits in reducing anxiety and stress. The bioactive constituents of Pogostemon quadrifolius include patchoulol, patchoulene, and other sesquiterpenes, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbalism, Pogostemon quadrifolius is commonly used in teas, tinctures, and essential oils, as well as being incorporated into topical creams and salves for skin care and wound healing.
